claims.claim_text: The December 2022 LastPass incident involved theft of encrypted customer vault data, and the later investigation tied the compromise to a senior DevOps engineer targeted through vulnerable third-party software.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.rationale: LastPass's own incident update appears to substantiate the technical chain, but the primary source is on an off-allowlist domain.

claims.claim_text: The breach affected 25 million users and caused an estimated $100 million in damages.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 1
claims.anchor_text: The breach affected 25 million users and resulted in an estimated $100 million in damages.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The breach affected millions of users and led to substantial legal, remediation, and reputational costs.
claims.rationale: The sentence combines precise impact numbers with no allowlisted primary source that directly backs both figures.

claims.claim_text: Azure Key Vault is a service for securely storing and managing secrets, cryptographic keys, and certificates.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Key Vault documentation directly describes the service as secure storage and management for keys, secrets, and certificates.

claims.claim_text: Azure Key Vault is backed by FIPS 140-2 Level 2 HSMs and integrates natively with virtually every Azure service.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, paragraph 3
claims.anchor_text: It is backed by FIPS 140-2 Level 2 validated hardware security modules (HSMs), and integrates natively with virtually every Azure service.
claims.suggested_rewrite: It supports HSM-protected keys and integrates with many Azure services, but the exact hardware validation level depends on the SKU and HSM platform.
claims.rationale: Current Microsoft docs distinguish between software-protected keys, multiple HSM platforms, and SKU-specific protection levels, and the 'virtually every Azure service' phrasing is not supportable.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault secrets can store values up to 25 KB.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Vault Fundamentals > The Three Object Types table, Secrets row
claims.rationale: Microsoft's secrets documentation explicitly states the 25 KB maximum size.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault object identifiers use the /secrets, /keys, and /certificates path prefixes.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Vault Fundamentals > The Three Object Types table, API Endpoint column
claims.rationale: The Key Vault object overview documents the object-type identifier suffixes for secrets, keys, and certificates.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault vaults support RSA and EC key types.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Vault Fundamentals > The Three Object Types table, Keys row
claims.rationale: The Key Vault keys documentation directly lists RSA and EC support for vaults.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault certificates manage X.509 certificates and can include private keys.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Vault Fundamentals > The Three Object Types table, Certificates row
claims.rationale: Microsoft's certificate documentation explicitly describes X.509 certificate management and retrieval behavior for private keys.

claims.claim_text: Updating a secret creates a new version, and the latest version becomes current while older versions remain addressable.
claims.span_hint: section: Secret Versioning
claims.rationale: The object overview documents Key Vault versioning semantics and retrieval by specific or latest version.

claims.claim_text: Applications can perform cryptographic operations with Key Vault keys without exposing the raw key material to the application.
claims.span_hint: section: Keys: Encryption Without Exposing Key Material
claims.rationale: Microsoft's developer guidance explicitly describes using keys for encryption and signing without exposing key material to the application.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault can integrate with DigiCert and GlobalSign and supports automatic renewal with selected issuers.
claims.span_hint: section: Certificates: Automated TLS Management
claims.rationale: Microsoft's certificate docs name DigiCert and GlobalSign as partner issuers and describe automatic renewal with selected issuers.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault supports automated rotation policies for keys.
claims.span_hint: section: Automated Secret Rotation > Key Vault Rotation Policies (Keys)
claims.rationale: Microsoft's key rotation documentation directly describes configuring automated rotation policies.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault emits a SecretNearExpiry event 30 days before a secret expires.
claims.span_hint: section: Automated Secret Rotation > Secret Rotation via Event Grid
claims.rationale: The Event Grid schema page explicitly states that SecretNearExpiry is triggered 30 days before expiration.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault supports two access control models, Azure RBAC and access policies, and Microsoft recommends RBAC.
claims.span_hint: section: Access Control: Access Policies vs Azure RBAC
claims.rationale: Microsoft's access-control comparison page explicitly documents the two models and recommends RBAC.

claims.claim_text: Access policies are configured at vault scope, while Azure RBAC supports separate permissions on individual keys, secrets, and certificates.
claims.span_hint: section: Access Control: Access Policies vs Azure RBAC
claims.rationale: The RBAC guide explicitly states that RBAC supports individual-resource scopes, while access policies are vault-level.

claims.claim_text: A company had 150 identities with vault-level secret access and all of them could read the production database password until the company migrated to RBAC.
claims.span_hint: section: Access Control: Access Policies vs Azure RBAC, War Story paragraph
claims.anchor_text: A company using Access Policies had 150 identities with vault-level secret access. When an audit asked "who can read the production database password specifically?", the answer was "all 150 identities."
claims.suggested_rewrite: With Access Policies, permissions are granted at vault scope, so a broad set of identities can end up with access to more secrets than they actually need.
claims.rationale: This is a quantified anecdote with no attributable source and should be rewritten as a general lesson.

claims.claim_text: Soft delete keeps deleted vault objects recoverable for a configurable retention period of 7 to 90 days.
claims.span_hint: section: Soft Delete and Purge Protection
claims.rationale: Microsoft's soft-delete overview explicitly documents the 7-90 day retention window.

claims.claim_text: Soft delete is mandatory for all vaults created after 2020.
claims.span_hint: section: Soft Delete and Purge Protection
claims.anchor_text: Soft delete is mandatory for all vaults created after 2020.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Soft delete is on by default for newly created key vaults.
claims.rationale: Current Microsoft docs support the present behavior for new vaults, not this date-specific historical statement.

claims.claim_text: With purge protection enabled, not even the vault owner or a Global Administrator can purge a soft-deleted object early.
claims.span_hint: section: Soft Delete and Purge Protection
claims.anchor_text: Not even the vault owner or a Global Administrator can purge it early.
claims.suggested_rewrite: When purge protection is enabled, a soft-deleted object cannot be purged until the retention period expires.
claims.rationale: The general no-early-purge rule is documented, but the sentence's named-role formulation is not directly stated in the Microsoft source.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault replicates data within the region and, in most paired regions, to a paired region; after Microsoft-managed regional failover the vault operates in read-only mode.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Region Architectures and Disaster Recovery
claims.rationale: Microsoft's reliability guidance explicitly documents paired-region replication and read-only behavior after failover.

claims.claim_text: If an application must continue writing secrets or managing keys during a regional outage, it needs separate key vaults in multiple regions rather than relying only on Microsoft-managed failover.
claims.span_hint: section: Multi-Region Architectures and Disaster Recovery > The Active-Active Vault Pattern
claims.rationale: Microsoft's reliability guidance recommends custom multi-region solutions with separate vaults when write capability across region failures is required.

claims.claim_text: DefaultAzureCredential can use local development credentials and then use managed identity when the same code runs in Azure.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating Key Vault with Applications > Pattern 1
claims.rationale: Microsoft's managed identity guidance explicitly states that DefaultAzureCredential works in local development and Azure-hosted managed-identity environments without code changes.

claims.claim_text: App Service, Functions, and Container Apps can all reference Key Vault secrets directly in application settings using the same model.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating Key Vault with Applications > Pattern 2
claims.anchor_text: Azure App Service, Functions, and Container Apps can reference Key Vault secrets directly in application settings, without any SDK code:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Azure App Service and Functions can use Key Vault references in app settings, and Container Apps can consume Key Vault-backed secrets without SDK code:
claims.rationale: The module currently collapses two related but different product mechanisms into one sentence.

claims.claim_text: For App Service and Functions, application code can read a Key Vault-backed setting like a normal app setting or connection string after Azure resolves the reference.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating Key Vault with Applications > Pattern 2, paragraph after CLI example
claims.rationale: Microsoft's App Service Key Vault references page explicitly says the app can use the setting like any other app setting or connection string.

claims.claim_text: Azure Container Apps can define secrets whose values come from Key Vault using a keyvaultref plus identityref pattern.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating Key Vault with Applications > Pattern 3
claims.rationale: The Container Apps secrets documentation directly shows Key Vault-backed secret definitions and the keyvaultref/identityref CLI syntax.

claims.claim_text: The Azure Key Vault provider for Secrets Store CSI Driver can mount Key Vault objects into AKS pods through a CSI volume.
claims.span_hint: section: Integrating Key Vault with Applications > Pattern 4
claims.rationale: Microsoft's AKS documentation explicitly describes mounting secrets, keys, and certificates into pods through the CSI driver.

claims.claim_text: By default, a newly created Key Vault has its firewall disabled, so requests can come from the public internet but still require authentication and authorization.
claims.span_hint: section: Key Vault Networking: Private and Secure
claims.rationale: Microsoft's Key Vault networking documentation explicitly states that the firewall is disabled by default and clarifies that authentication and permissions still apply.

claims.claim_text: Azure Key Vault processes over 200 billion transactions per month across all Azure customers as of 2024.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 1
claims.anchor_text: **Azure Key Vault processes over 200 billion transactions per month** across all Azure customers as of 2024.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Azure Key Vault handles a very large volume of requests** across Azure customers.
claims.rationale: No allowlisted Microsoft source surfaced for this exact global transaction figure.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault Premium uses FIPS 140-2 Level 3 HSMs, Standard uses Level 2, Level 3 is required for some PCI-DSS interpretations, and Premium costs about $1 per key per month while Standard operations cost about $0.03 per 10,000 operations.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 2
claims.anchor_text: **Key Vault Premium SKU uses FIPS 140-2 Level 3 validated HSMs** (Marvell LiquidSecurity), while Standard uses Level 2. The difference: Level 3 HSMs have physical tamper-evidence mechanisms and identity-based authentication. Level 3 is required for certain compliance frameworks like PCI-DSS in some interpretations. Premium costs approximately $1 per key per month, while Standard key operations are billed per 10,000 operations ($0.03).
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Key Vault tiers differ in protection model and pricing**. Check the current Microsoft documentation and pricing page before making compliance or cost claims about a specific SKU.
claims.rationale: This bullet mixes outdated FIPS-version language, a compliance interpretation claim, and time-sensitive pricing that is not safely supportable as written.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault has a limit of 4,000 non-create secret and vault transactions per vault per region per 10 seconds.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 3
claims.rationale: Microsoft's service-limits page explicitly documents the 4,000 transactions per 10 seconds threshold.

claims.claim_text: A team with 500 microservices, each fetching 3 secrets at startup, hit throttling and fixed it by refreshing every 5 minutes and staggering deployments.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 3
claims.anchor_text: A team running 500 microservices that each fetched 3 secrets at startup experienced throttling when deploying all services simultaneously. The fix: cache secrets locally with a reasonable refresh interval (every 5 minutes instead of every request) and stagger deployments.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Large microservice rollouts can hit Key Vault throttling if every instance fetches multiple secrets at once. Cache secrets locally, refresh them on a sensible interval, and stagger large deployments.
claims.rationale: The exact numbers read like an illustrative teaching example rather than a sourced case study, but the underlying throttling lesson is sound.

claims.claim_text: A team enabled purge protection on a test vault with a 90-day retention period and therefore could not fully clean up deleted test secrets for 3 months.
claims.span_hint: section: Did You Know? item 4
claims.anchor_text: A team accidentally enabled purge protection on a test vault with a 90-day retention period, meaning they cannot fully clean up deleted test secrets for 3 months.
claims.suggested_rewrite: If you enable purge protection on a test vault with a long retention period, deleted objects can't be permanently purged until that retention window expires.
claims.rationale: The exact scenario is presented as an anecdote, but the teaching point about retention windows and purge protection is directly supported.

claims.claim_text: A common organizational pattern is to use one vault per application and environment.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, rows 'Creating one vault per secret' and 'Use one vault per application/environment'
claims.rationale: Microsoft's current application guidance explicitly recommends separate key vaults for different applications and environments.

claims.claim_text: RBAC provides per-secret scoping, Conditional Access integration, and PIM support.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Using Access Policies instead of RBAC on new vaults'
claims.anchor_text: RBAC provides per-secret scoping, Conditional Access integration, and PIM support.
claims.suggested_rewrite: RBAC provides per-secret scoping and uses standard Azure role-assignment and privileged-access workflows.
claims.rationale: Per-resource scoping is directly supported, but this sentence overstates the surrounding identity-feature integrations without a clean single-source match.

claims.claim_text: Each Key Vault call adds 5-10 ms latency.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Reading secrets from Key Vault on every request'
claims.anchor_text: Key Vault has throttling limits, and each call adds 5-10ms latency.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Key Vault has throttling limits, and each call adds network latency.
claims.rationale: The precise latency figure is not well anchored in allowlisted docs, but the general performance lesson is valid.

claims.claim_text: Key Vault has built-in rotation policies in preview for secrets.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Not rotating secrets'
claims.anchor_text: Implement secret rotation. Use Key Vault's built-in rotation policies (preview), or use Event Grid notifications to trigger rotation logic.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Implement secret rotation. Use Key Vault rotation policies for keys, or Event Grid notifications to trigger custom secret-rotation logic.
claims.rationale: As written, the sentence blurs key rotation policy support with secret-rotation workflows and includes stale preview framing.

claims.claim_text: The Key Vault Administrator role can perform all data-plane operations, while Key Vault Secrets User is a read-only secret-consumption role.
claims.span_hint: section: Common Mistakes, row 'Granting Key Vault Administrator when Key Vault Secrets User is sufficient'
claims.rationale: Microsoft's RBAC guide explicitly defines the built-in Key Vault Administrator and Key Vault Secrets User roles.

further_reading.title: Azure Key Vault Developer's Guide
further_reading.why_relevant: Good primary overview for how Key Vault stores secrets, uses keys, and manages certificates.

further_reading.title: Provide Access to Key Vault with Azure RBAC
further_reading.why_relevant: Covers built-in roles, scope granularity, and the current recommended access-control model.

further_reading.title: Azure Key Vault Soft-Delete Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Directly backs the module's soft delete, purge protection, retention, and recovery content.

further_reading.title: Reliability in Azure Key Vault
further_reading.why_relevant: Best primary source for paired-region replication, failover behavior, and custom multi-region guidance.

notes: Excluded quiz items, quiz answers, exercise steps, code blocks, and Mermaid diagrams per instructions. Several 'Did You Know' and 'Common Mistakes' bullets combine sound guidance with brittle numbers, dated pricing, or unsourced anecdotes; the rewrite dispositions above preserve the lesson while removing unsupported specifics.
